Tehran claims it destroyed the US Fifth Fleet's command centre — unverified
Trump: 'Next week comes the power plants... the bridges' unless Iran negotiates
US reimposes its naval blockade of Iran's ports; fourth straight day of fire
stories.moot: Wars escalate along two tracks at once — who gets hit and what gets hit. When a conflict spreads from proxies to a rival's own bases, and from military targets to civilian infrastructure like power and bridges, it is widening on both fronts, which is how a contained fight becomes a regional one. And every step up keeps a fear premium under the oil price, even before a single extra barrel is actually lost.

thread.previously: We reported the US dropping its 20% Hormuz toll but keeping a naval blockade of Iran's ships, a chemical tanker hit off Oman, and Yemen's Houthis striking Saudi Arabia's Abha airport as the war widened across the Gulf.
thread.changed: Iran has now struck US bases directly — in Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan — claiming (unverified) to have destroyed the Fifth Fleet's command centre, and Trump has threatened to bomb Iran's power plants and bridges 'next week,' pushing the war onto US allies and toward civilian infrastructure.

depth.background: The US–Iran war has now run for more than four months, and a ceasefire signed in June has collapsed back into open fighting centred on the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea lane that carries about a fifth of the world's oil. Until now, most of the fire aimed at the US came from Iran's regional allies, like Yemen's Houthis. What is new is Iran hitting American forces directly, inside friendly Gulf countries: Bahrain hosts the headquarters of the US Navy's Fifth Fleet (the fleet that patrols the Gulf), Kuwait hosts the Ali Al Salem airbase, and Jordan hosts the Azraq airbase. Those host states had tried to stay on the sidelines.
depth.why_it_matters: Two lines are being crossed at once. Iran is now striking US bases inside allied Gulf states rather than acting only through proxies, which threatens to pull Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan into the war and risks a far wider regional conflict. And Trump is publicly threatening to target Iran's civilian infrastructure — power plants and bridges — which pushes the war beyond military sites and raises grave humanitarian and legal questions. The more the fighting widens and the more infrastructure is put in the crosshairs, the greater the threat to oil supply and the global economy.
depth.ripple_effects: Direct hits on US bases can force a larger American military response and drag in the Gulf states that host US forces, making any return to negotiations harder. Threatening power plants and bridges shifts the pressure onto Iran's economy and daily life, which could harden Iranian resolve as easily as it forces concessions. For markets, each escalation keeps a war-risk premium under oil, pressures oil-importers like India (a weaker rupee, stickier inflation, a bigger fuel bill) and lifts energy and defence names while squeezing airlines, shipping and other fuel-heavy businesses. A genuine closure of Hormuz remains the single biggest cross-asset tail risk.
depth.why_now: Days of US air strikes failed to force Iran to stand down, so Tehran widened its retaliation from proxies to US bases across the Gulf, while Washington reinstated its blockade and Trump ratcheted up his threats — each side escalating to try to force the other to the table (or to back off) after June's ceasefire fell apart.
depth.watch_next: Whether the US retaliates for the strikes on its bases, whether Trump follows through on hitting Iran's power plants and bridges next week, and whether Brent breaks decisively above $90.
depth.market_link: Keeps a war-risk bid under Brent (near a one-month high around $86) and pressures oil-importers and the rupee; energy and defence names are relative winners while airlines, shipping and other fuel-heavy sectors get squeezed — a full Hormuz closure stays the biggest tail risk.

key_terms.definition: The US Navy fleet based in Bahrain that patrols the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz; its core job is to keep those waters — and the oil that flows through them — open.
Iran's elite military force, separate from its regular army; it runs the country's missile and drone programmes and its operations across the region.
Using warships to physically stop vessels reaching or leaving a country's ports; here it chokes Iran's oil exports and risks direct clashes at sea.
Facilities like power plants and bridges that serve the general population; striking them, rather than only military targets, sharply raises the human and legal stakes of a war.

sources.name: BBC World (Trump threatens to bomb bridges and power plants unless Iran resumes talks)
Al Jazeera (Iran launches attacks across region as US strikes continue)
ANI (Trump threatens strikes on Iranian power plants, bridges 'next week')
ABC News (Iran live updates: US carries out latest strikes, resumes naval blockade)

stories.title: Files on India's biggest nuclear plant, Kudankulam, surface on the dark web after a contractor is hacked — including alleged blueprints
stories.what_happened: A ransomware and extortion group calling itself 'World Leaks' posted a large cache of files on the dark web that it says relates to Kudankulam, including purported blueprints of parts of the facility and supplier details. The group claims the files are part of a much larger set of roughly 858,000 files taken from Reliance Group, one of the plant's contractors, and says it has published more than 19,000 of them. Reliance Group, controlled by industrialist Anil Ambani, told Reuters there was a 'partial breach' of its data on a server hosted by third-party Indian data-centre provider Yotta, and that the government has been informed, but did not say exactly what was taken.
key_points: Ransomware gang 'World Leaks' dumped 19,000+ files on the dark web
Data allegedly stolen from contractor Anil Ambani's Reliance Group
Files reportedly include blueprints of parts of the Kudankulam plant
CERT-In is investigating; a nuclear expert calls the risk 'serious'
stories.moot: Your security is only as strong as your weakest supplier — attackers increasingly breach the small contractor or data-centre vendor rather than the well-guarded main target, because sensitive data flows to those third parties too. Guarding the 'front door' of a critical facility means little if its blueprints sit on a poorly secured vendor's server.

depth.background: The Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant (KKNPP) in Tamil Nadu is India's largest, built with Russian help, and a flagship of the country's atomic-energy programme. It has been a cyber target before: in 2019 malware linked to a North Korean hacking group was found on the plant's administrative network. Nuclear facilities rely on a web of outside contractors and suppliers for construction and parts — and those third parties are often the weakest link in security.
depth.why_it_matters: Even without a direct attack on the reactor's controls, leaked blueprints and supplier data can hand hostile actors a map of a critical facility — its layout, weak points and supply chain — which experts warn could aid physical sabotage or a future cyber-attack. It is a stark reminder that a nation's most sensitive infrastructure is only as secure as the private vendors it hands data to.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect an urgent government and CERT-In review of how contractors store sensitive nuclear and defence data, tighter rules on third-party data centres, and reputational and possibly legal pressure on Reliance Group and Yotta. Other Indian critical-infrastructure operators — power grids, ports, defence suppliers — will be pushed to audit their own contractor security. It also feeds a wider global worry about ransomware gangs targeting energy and nuclear assets.
depth.why_now: The files were posted to the dark web and the breach was first reported by Reuters on 15 July 2026, with the ransomware group actively publishing the data as leverage; it surfaces amid a rising wave of ransomware attacks on infrastructure and contractors worldwide.
depth.watch_next: Whether CERT-In and the Nuclear Power Corporation of India confirm what specifically was exposed and whether any safety-critical or classified material is among it, plus any regulatory action against the contractor.
depth.market_link: Sentiment risk for Anil Ambani's Reliance Group entities and the data-centre provider Yotta; a broader breach could lift attention on Indian cybersecurity firms and critical-infrastructure security spending.

key_terms.definition: Criminal hackers who steal or lock up an organisation's data and threaten to publish it unless they are paid; 'World Leaks' is the group claiming this breach.
A hidden part of the internet reachable only with special software, where stolen data is often leaked or sold anonymously.
The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team — the government's main cybersecurity agency that responds to and investigates hacking incidents.
The danger that sensitive data is exposed not by the main organisation but through an outside vendor or supplier it shares data with.

stories.title: Japan just moved crypto into the same rulebook as stocks and bonds — clearing the path for Bitcoin ETFs and a much lower tax
stories.what_happened: On July 15 Japan's upper house of parliament, the House of Councillors, gave final approval to a law that reclassifies cryptocurrencies as 'financial products' and shifts their oversight from the Payment Services Act to the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (FIEA) — the same core law that governs stocks and bonds. The change takes effect in 2027. Japan's Financial Services Agency will now treat crypto as a regulated investment: with insider-trading bans, disclosure requirements, stronger investor protection, and bigger penalties for unregistered operators. Lawmakers separately backed a plan to cut the top tax on crypto gains from as high as 55% to a flat 20%, starting 2028.
key_points: Parliament reclassifies crypto as a regulated 'financial product'
Opens the door to spot Bitcoin ETFs for ordinary investors
Top tax on crypto gains set to fall from up to 55% to a flat 20%
New insider-trading bans and disclosure rules; takes effect 2027
stories.moot: How an asset is legally classified shapes everything around it — the tax you pay, who is allowed to sell it to you, and what products can be built on top. Moving crypto from a 'payments' law to an 'investments' law is what turns a fringe token into something a pension fund or a cautious saver can actually hold.

depth.background: For years Japan treated crypto mainly as a 'means of payment' under its Payment Services Act — a law written to police money transfers, not investments. That left Japanese crypto traders facing some of the world's harshest taxes (gains treated as 'miscellaneous income' at rates up to 55%) and gave regulators no clean legal route to approve mainstream investment products. Japan was one of the earliest crypto hubs and the scene of huge exchange hacks (Mt. Gox, Coincheck), so it has long wrestled with how to supervise the asset.
depth.why_it_matters: One of the world's largest economies is now formally treating crypto as a legitimate, regulated investment class rather than a fringe payment tool. That legal status is exactly what makes mainstream products possible — most importantly a 'spot Bitcoin ETF', a fund you can buy in an ordinary brokerage account that holds real Bitcoin, so regular savers get exposure without ever handling the coins. Clear rules plus a far lower tax can pull cautious retail and institutional money off the sidelines.
depth.ripple_effects: If Japan's regulator goes on to approve spot crypto ETFs, it joins the US (which launched them in 2024) in giving digital assets a regulated on-ramp for everyday savers and big institutions — potentially channeling household and pension-style money into the asset. It pressures other Asian markets like South Korea and Hong Kong to keep pace. For crypto firms, clearer rules cut legal uncertainty but raise compliance costs, and unregistered players face stiffer penalties. The flat 20% tax (versus up to 55%) could also stem the flight of Japanese traders and startups to friendlier countries.
depth.why_now: Japan has been overhauling its crypto rules for over a year; the bill cleared the lower house and committees last month and won final upper-house approval today. It is part of a global race among governments to both regulate and court a fast-growing digital-asset industry.
depth.watch_next: Whether Japan's Financial Services Agency formally approves the country's first spot Bitcoin ETF once the law takes effect in 2027 — the concrete signal that regulated crypto funds are open to Japanese investors.
depth.market_link: Bullish for Bitcoin and major crypto, and for exchanges and fund issuers eyeing Japanese ETFs; Japan-listed crypto-exposed and brokerage stocks could benefit as regulated products open up.

key_terms.definition: Japan's core investor-protection law governing stocks, bonds and other securities; putting crypto under it means treating it like a regulated investment.
An exchange-traded fund that holds actual Bitcoin, letting people invest through a normal brokerage account without buying or storing the coins themselves.
Buying or selling an asset using important information that isn't public yet — banned in regulated markets because it is unfair to other investors.

stories.title: India's car market just had its best-ever start to a year: a record 12.74 lakh vehicles shipped in Q1, up ~26%
stories.what_happened: Industry body SIAM (Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers) said passenger-vehicle dispatches hit a record 12,73,811 units (about 12.74 lakh) in the first quarter of FY27 (April-June 2026), up 25.9% from 10,11,884 a year earlier. SIAM said passenger vehicles, commercial vehicles and three-wheelers all logged their highest-ever first-quarter sales; commercial-vehicle dispatches rose 18.3% to about 2.65 lakh units. SIAM President Shailesh Chandra credited lower GST rates, softer financing costs, a low base a year ago, and a wave of new model launches.
key_points: Passenger-vehicle dispatches hit a record 12,73,811 units in Q1 FY27
Up 25.9% from a year ago; cars, trucks and three-wheelers all set Q1 records
SIAM credits GST cuts, cheaper loans, new models and a low base
A rare upbeat signal amid high inflation and Gulf oil-price risk
stories.moot: Car sales are a window into the wider economy: because most vehicles are bought on credit, a strong quarter usually means households feel confident and loans are cheap. But watch the gap between factory 'dispatches' and real buyers — shipping cars to dealers is not the same as selling them.

depth.background: Passenger vehicles — cars, SUVs and vans — are one of the clearest gauges of how confident Indian households feel about spending, because most are bought on loans. India's car market cooled through parts of 2024-25 as high prices and costly borrowing bit. The government responded by cutting GST (the Goods and Services Tax, India's nationwide sales tax) on many vehicles, and the RBI (India's central bank) lowered interest rates to make car loans cheaper. One caveat to read the numbers correctly: these are 'dispatches' — vehicles shipped from factories to dealers — not final retail sales to buyers.
depth.why_it_matters: A car is a big-ticket purchase most families finance, so a record quarter signals that consumer demand — the biggest engine of India's economy — is holding up even as inflation ticks higher and the West Asia (Iran-Hormuz) conflict pushes up oil prices. Autos also ripple far and wide: the sector underpins millions of jobs across steel, tyres, electronics, dealerships and lending.
depth.ripple_effects: Strong dispatches lift automakers and their vast supplier base — parts, steel, tyres, glass, chips — plus the banks and NBFCs (non-bank lenders) that write car loans. But dispatches can run ahead of actual buyers: if showroom (retail) sales don't keep pace, dealers pile up unsold stock and are forced into discounts later. SIAM expects steady festive-season demand — India's big buying stretch around September-November — to carry the momentum into the next quarter.
depth.why_now: SIAM has just released the Q1 (April-June) data, and it lands as a rare piece of clearly upbeat economic news against a gloomier backdrop of rising retail inflation, a rupee near record lows past 96 to the dollar, and oil-price risk from the Gulf conflict.
depth.watch_next: Whether the festive season (September-November) delivers the demand SIAM is banking on — and whether retail (actual buyer) sales keep pace with these record factory dispatches.
depth.market_link: Positive for Indian auto stocks (Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra & Mahindra, Hyundai India) and the Nifty Auto index, plus parts suppliers and auto financiers.

key_terms.definition: Vehicles shipped from factories to dealers — a measure of production and supply, which can differ from how many cars real buyers actually drive home.
India's single nationwide tax on most goods and services; cutting it on vehicles lowers the sticker price and can boost demand.
The Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers, the industry body that compiles India's official vehicle-sales data.

stories.source: Al Jazeera (India's ethanol rush prompts anger among vehicle owners, questions for gov't)
stories.title: India's E20 petrol backlash boils over: drivers say the mandatory 20% ethanol blend cuts mileage and harms older engines
stories.what_happened: The rollout has triggered mounting public anger, now spilling into politics and the courts. Owners of older cars and two-wheelers complain of falling mileage and problems like rough idling, hard starts and clogged filters, arguing their engines were never built for so much ethanol. Protesters gathered at Delhi's Jantar Mantar on July 5 under the slogan 'Humari Gaadi, Humara Adhikaar' ('Our vehicle, our right'), demanding a choice of fuel. Opposition leader Arvind Kejriwal escalated it, writing on July 8 to 29 automakers — reading aloud from Maruti and Toyota owner's manuals that advise E10 only — and asking them to guarantee in writing that they will compensate customers if mileage drops more than 10%; Maruti and Toyota did not immediately respond. On the other side, Nagpur's cyber police filed a case on July 15 against social-media users over alleged fake and defamatory posts targeting road transport minister Nitin Gadkari, a leading champion of ethanol, showing how politically charged the fight has become.
key_points: Since April 1, only E20 (20% ethanol) petrol is sold at India's ~90,000 pumps
Owners of pre-2023 cars and bikes report lower mileage and engine trouble
Kejriwal wrote to 29 carmakers demanding compensation for any mileage drop
Government pushes back — Nagpur police file an FIR over 'fake' posts on the policy
stories.moot: A policy can be right for the country and still hurt the individual — and that gap is where politics happens. Cutting oil imports with ethanol helps the national balance sheet, but forcing one fuel on a fleet built for another shifts the cost onto ordinary owners as worse mileage and wear. When a reform removes choice overnight, the backlash is often about the 'how', not the goal.

depth.background: Ethanol is alcohol made from crops such as sugarcane and grain; blending it into petrol lets a country burn less imported oil, supports farmers, and can modestly cut some emissions. India imports about 85% of its crude oil, so every litre of homegrown ethanol saves precious foreign currency — which is why New Delhi has pushed 'blending' hard for years, raising the ethanol share step by step. From April 1, 2026, the government made E20 — petrol with 20% ethanol — the only grade sold at the country's roughly 90,000 fuel stations. The catch: vehicles built before about April 2023, when E20-ready models began selling, were designed for petrol with only 5-10% ethanol.
depth.why_it_matters: This is a clash between a real national goal and real consumer pain. The policy genuinely cuts India's oil-import bill and helps sugarcane farmers — but by making E20 the only option overnight, the government removed choice for hundreds of millions of existing vehicle owners whose engines were built for weaker blends. When a green or self-reliance policy lands as higher running costs and possible engine damage for ordinary people, it becomes a political flashpoint — exactly what is happening now.
depth.ripple_effects: Lower mileage effectively raises the cost of every tankful, hitting household budgets and stoking the same fuel-price frustration that already weighs on India amid a weak rupee and a costly oil-import bill. The row is a tailwind for sugar and distillery companies that produce ethanol, but a reputational headache for carmakers caught between the government's mandate and customers' compatibility complaints. Politically, it hands the opposition a pocketbook issue and pressures the government to consider offering a lower-ethanol option or clearer compatibility guidance.
depth.why_now: The mandate took full effect on April 1, but the backlash has crested this month as drivers logged a summer of worse mileage, protests hit Jantar Mantar, Kejriwal's letters forced carmakers into the spotlight, and the government's own supporters began filing police complaints against critics — all trending together on July 15.
depth.watch_next: Whether the government offers a non-E20 or lower-blend option, how (or whether) carmakers respond to Kejriwal's compensation demand, and any court or regulatory intervention on fuel compatibility.
depth.market_link: The ethanol push supports sugar and distillery names (e.g. Balrampur Chini, Triveni, EID Parry, and ethanol-tech firm Praj) and trims the oil-import bill that pressures the rupee, while the compatibility row is a mild overhang for auto and after-sales sentiment.

key_terms.definition: Mixing ethanol (crop-based alcohol) into petrol; 'E20' means 20% ethanol and 80% petrol. Higher blends cut oil imports but can harm engines not designed for them.
The total money a country spends buying crude oil from abroad; India imports ~85% of its oil, so ethanol that replaces petrol saves foreign currency and narrows the trade gap.
Whether an engine is built to run safely on a given fuel; vehicles made before ~2023 were tuned for E5-E10, so E20 can lower their mileage and stress parts.

sources.name: Al Jazeera (India's ethanol rush prompts anger among vehicle owners, questions for gov't)
Reuters via Yahoo (Backlash on ethanol-blend fuel intensifies in India, puts carmakers in the dock)
Outlook Business (E20 Fuel Row: Kejriwal Writes to 29 Auto Firms Demanding Clarity on Engine Damage, Compensation)
LiveMint (FIR filed over alleged fake social media campaign targeting Nitin Gadkari on E20 fuel)

stories.title: Tata wants to build ships in Kerala: the group behind Tata Steel and Jaguar seeks approval for a ₹10,000 crore ($1bn) shipyard — its first-ever move into shipbuilding
stories.what_happened: At the Kerala Samudra Mission 2026 seminar in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala Chief Minister V.D. Satheesan said the Tata Group has sought the state's approval for a ₹10,000 crore (about $1 billion) shipbuilding investment. He said the state would provide the land and is likely to clear the proposal within a month. Satheesan also revealed that Singapore's Changi Airport — one of the world's top-rated airports — is interested in aviation-related investment in Kerala. The shipbuilding plan would mark Tata's first entry into the sector.
key_points: Tata seeks state approval for a ₹10,000 crore ($1bn) Kerala shipyard
It would be Tata's first-ever entry into shipbuilding
Kerala says it will provide land and may approve within a month
Part of Kerala's 'Mission Samudra' port-led economy push
stories.moot: A country can dominate the *use* of an industry while barely participating in *making* it — India moves enormous volumes of goods by sea yet builds almost none of the ships. Closing that kind of gap needs patient, capital-heavy players, which is why a conglomerate's balance sheet, not a startup's, tends to be what finally enters industries like shipbuilding.

depth.background: India is a shipping giant that barely builds its own ships. It relies on the sea for most of its trade and imports most of its oil, yet Indian shipyards make under 1% of the world's ships, while China, South Korea and Japan together build roughly 90%. New Delhi has spent years trying to change that with subsidies and a 'Maritime India' plan, wanting home-built ships for trade, the navy, and jobs. Tata, India's largest conglomerate, already spans steel, cars (it owns Jaguar Land Rover), software, power and consumer goods — but has never built ships.
depth.why_it_matters: If it goes ahead, a Tata shipyard would put one of India's most trusted industrial names — with deep pockets and engineering muscle — behind the country's long-stalled ambition to build ships at home. That matters for self-reliance (fewer vessels bought from China or Korea), for defence (naval and coast-guard ships), for skilled jobs in a coastal state, and for cutting India's import bill. It is also a vote of confidence in Kerala as an industrial destination, not just a tourism one.
depth.ripple_effects: A big new domestic yard would create thousands of skilled jobs and pull in a supply chain of steel, engines and components — a natural fit with Tata Steel. It would also raise the competitive bar for India's existing shipbuilders like Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock and Garden Reach, even as it grows the overall industry. For Kerala, landing both Tata and Changi would be a signal flare to other investors. But this is still a proposal: land, environmental clearances and a firm capital commitment all have to follow the headline.
depth.why_now: Kerala has just launched 'Mission Samudra,' a ₹400 crore plan to turn its long coastline and new deep-water Vizhinjam port into a maritime economic hub — and it is actively courting marquee investors like Tata to anchor it. Globally, shipbuyers are also hunting for capacity outside China, which opens a rare window for new entrants.
depth.watch_next: Whether the Kerala cabinet formally approves the proposal within the promised month — and whether Tata publicly confirms the plan and names a site.
depth.market_link: Watch Indian shipbuilders — Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Garden Reach — and Tata group stocks such as Tata Steel; a credible new entrant reshapes expectations for the entire sector.

key_terms.definition: A single company that owns many unrelated businesses at once — Tata, for example, spans steel, cars, software, salt and power.
Kerala's state initiative (samudra means 'ocean') to build a port-led economy, using its coastline and the new Vizhinjam deep-water port to attract maritime industry.
The backlog of ships a yard has already been contracted to build — the industry's main gauge of future work and market share.

stories.source: Reuters via Yahoo Finance (Indian investigators conducted psychological autopsy in Air India crash probe, court filing says)
stories.title: India's Air India crash probe enters its final stage: investigators finish the cockpit-voice transcript and a 'psychological autopsy,' with the draft report due in October
stories.what_happened: In a court filing and to India's Supreme Court, the AAIB said its investigation has moved into its final stages. It has completed a transcript of the cockpit voice recorder (the 'black box' that captures cockpit audio) and carried out a 'psychological autopsy' — a reconstruction of the crew's mental state and history — and has interviewed pilots, other crew who had flown with them, technical staff, air-traffic controllers and weather officials. Still pending are an analysis of data pulled in late May from an engine-monitoring unit and a review of organisational factors; roughly six more weeks of analysis are expected. A draft final report is anticipated around October and will be shared with participating countries, including the US National Transportation Safety Board, for comment before public release. The AAIB also told the court the cockpit recordings themselves cannot be shared publicly; separately, the captain's father has sued over what he says was an implication his son deliberately cut the fuel.
key_points: Probe into the June 2025 Ahmedabad Boeing 787 crash (260 dead) moves to final analysis
Investigators have completed a cockpit-voice-recorder transcript and a 'psychological autopsy' of the crew
Draft final report expected around October; to be shared with the US NTSB before release
India's AAIB told the Supreme Court the cockpit audio itself cannot be made public
stories.moot: Air-crash investigations are deliberately slow and layered — machines, procedures and people are all examined before any cause is named. Early leaks and speculation almost always outrun the evidence, which is exactly why final reports take a year or more and why an unproven theory should never be treated as the conclusion.

depth.background: On June 12, 2025, an Air India Boeing 787 Dreamliner (flight AI171) bound for London Gatwick crashed seconds after taking off from Ahmedabad, killing 260 people — one of the deadliest air disasters in India's history. The Aircraft Accident Investigation Bureau (AAIB) is the independent government body that investigates such crashes. A preliminary report last year noted that the engines' fuel-control switches moved to the 'cutoff' position just after takeoff — a detail that triggered intense but unproven public speculation about the crew, which the captain's family firmly rejects. Under global aviation rules, no cause is official until the final report is published.
depth.why_it_matters: 260 people died, so the eventual finding carries enormous weight. Whether the cause is judged to be a technical fault, human factors, or a mix will shape scrutiny of Boeing's 787 worldwide, the rebuilding of Air India under the Tata Group, and India's safety credibility just as it becomes one of the world's fastest-growing aviation markets. The inclusion of a 'psychological autopsy' signals investigators are formally weighing human factors — but crucially, no cause has been officially concluded, and unverified speculation should not be mistaken for a verdict.
depth.ripple_effects: A finding that points to a technical or design fault would ripple out to Boeing's global 787 fleet, regulators and airlines; a human-factors conclusion would instead push the industry toward tighter pilot-monitoring and mental-health protocols. Either way it affects Air India's insurers and Tata's turnaround, the compensation owed to victims' families, and public trust in flying in India. Because the report is shared with the US NTSB, its conclusions will also be scrutinised by American regulators and Boeing.
depth.why_now: The status surfaced on July 14–15 through a Supreme Court hearing and fresh reporting, about 13 months after the crash, as the probe crossed from evidence-gathering into its final analysis phase and the AAIB set out its timeline to the court.
depth.watch_next: The draft final report expected around October — and whether it attributes the crash to a technical fault, human factors, or a combination.
depth.market_link: Limited direct market impact, but it keeps scrutiny on Boeing (BA) and its 787 programme and weighs on the Tata Group's rebuilding of the unlisted Air India.

key_terms.definition: India's Aircraft Accident Investigation Bureau — the independent government body that investigates air crashes and publishes the official report on what caused them.
A crash-hardened device that records cockpit audio and conversations; investigators transcribe it to reconstruct the final minutes, but the raw audio is usually kept confidential.

A structured after-the-fact review of a person's mental state, stresses and history — used in investigations to assess whether human factors may have played a role, without on its own proving a cause.
The cockpit switch that lets fuel flow to an engine ('run') or shuts it off ('cutoff'); the preliminary report noted these moved to cutoff after takeoff, a finding still being investigated.

sources.name: Reuters via Yahoo Finance (Indian investigators conducted psychological autopsy in Air India crash probe, court filing says)
Reuters via TradingView (Air India crash probe enters final stages after cockpit recorder, psychology reviews)
Oman Observer (Air India B787 crash probe enters final stages)

stories.source: Reuters (Exclusive: Stripe, Advent offer to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion, sources say)
stories.title: Stripe and a private-equity giant make a surprise $53bn-plus bid for PayPal — a payments pioneer under siege
stories.what_happened: According to a Reuters exclusive confirmed by Bloomberg and CNBC, Stripe and Advent have jointly offered to buy PayPal at a valuation above $53 billion — $60.50 per share, roughly a 28% premium to where PayPal closed on Tuesday. The offer is said to be backed by about $50 billion of committed financing from banks, and the two buyers would take equal 50-50 ownership and keep the company whole rather than break it up. Crucially, this is an approach, not a done deal: the suitors first reached out in early April, PayPal has not yet formally responded, and they are trying to push talks forward in the coming weeks. PayPal's stock jumped around 15% after the news leaked, as traders bet a bidding process could follow.
key_points: Stripe and Advent International offer to buy PayPal at a $53bn-plus valuation
Bid is $60.50 a share — about a 28% premium over PayPal's Tuesday close
Backed by ~$50bn of committed bank financing; the two would own it 50-50
It's an unsolicited approach — PayPal hasn't responded, and no deal is agreed yet
stories.moot: A big takeover premium is the market telling you a company was cheaper than it was worth. When a challenger and a private-equity firm team up to bid far above the share price, it means outsiders see value the public market had overlooked — and a single M&A headline can re-rate an entire sector in a day. But an 'offer' is not a deal: until the target's board agrees, it's a proposal, not a done transaction.

depth.background: PayPal is one of the internet's original payment companies — spun out of the eBay era, it lets people and businesses send money and pay online, and for years it was the default 'pay' button on the web. But newer rivals have crowded in: Stripe (a fast-growing, still-private company whose software lets businesses accept card payments) and Apple Pay, plus 'buy now, pay later' apps, have eaten into PayPal's growth, and its share price has lagged. Advent International is one of the world's big private-equity firms — an investor that buys whole companies, usually with borrowed money, aiming to improve them and sell later at a profit. A 'takeover bid' is simply an offer by one party to buy control of another company by purchasing its shares.
depth.why_it_matters: This would be one of the largest deals ever in financial technology, and it signals a shift in the payments industry: a younger, private challenger (Stripe) teaming with deep-pocketed private-equity money to swallow the incumbent that once defined online payments. If it succeeds, it reshapes who controls the plumbing behind a huge slice of internet commerce. The size of the premium — 28% — also tells you buyers think PayPal is worth far more than the public market had been pricing it at.
depth.ripple_effects: A live takeover battle tends to lift not just the target's shares but those of similar companies, as investors re-rate the whole payments and fintech sector on hopes of more deals. A combined Stripe-PayPal would be a payments powerhouse facing tougher antitrust scrutiny, since regulators worry when two big players in the same business merge. It also shows private-equity firms are willing to write $50-billion cheques again — a sign that cheaper money and steadier markets are reviving mega-deals after a quiet stretch. For consumers and merchants, ownership changes could eventually affect fees and which 'pay' buttons dominate checkout.
depth.why_now: The offer surfaced publicly on July 15 via a Reuters report, but it builds on an approach the buyers first made in early April; the suitors appear to be going public — or letting the news out — to pressure PayPal's board into engaging after months without a formal answer.
depth.watch_next: Whether PayPal's board rejects, engages, or seeks rival bidders, and whether antitrust regulators signal concern about combining two major payments players.
depth.market_link: PayPal (PYPL) shares surged ~15% on the report and could stay volatile through any bidding process; the news re-rates the broader payments and fintech sector, while Stripe stays private and Advent supplies the private-equity firepower.

key_terms.definition: An offer to buy control of a company by purchasing its shares; the 'premium' is how much more than the current market price the buyer offers — here about 28% — to persuade shareholders to sell.
Investment firms (like Advent) that buy whole companies, usually funded largely by borrowed money, aiming to improve and later resell them for profit.
Money that banks have formally promised to lend to fund a deal, so the buyer can show it actually has the cash to complete a purchase this large.
A takeover offer made without the target company first inviting it; the target's board can reject it, negotiate, or look for a better buyer.

sources.name: Reuters (Exclusive: Stripe, Advent offer to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion, sources say)
CNBC (Stripe, Advent offer to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion: Reuters)
Bloomberg (Stripe, Advent Offer to Buy PayPal for $53 Billion, Reuters Says)

stories.title: China's economy grows just 4.3% — its slowest pace since 2022 — as a slump in investment and cautious shoppers drag on the world's No. 2 economy
stories.what_happened: On July 15, 2026, China's statistics bureau reported that the economy grew 4.3% in the second quarter (April-June) compared with a year earlier. That undershot economists' forecasts of about 4.5% and marked a steep cooling from the 5% growth of the first quarter — the slowest quarterly reading since the lockdown-hit final months of 2022. Growth for the first half of the year came in at 4.7%. The weakness was concentrated in investment, which slid as factories and local governments held back on new projects, while retail spending stayed subdued. One bright spot: exports jumped 27% in June (and 17.6% across the first half), lifted by booming global demand for Chinese electric vehicles and AI-related electronics — without that export surge, the slowdown would have been worse.
key_points: April-June GDP grew 4.3% from a year earlier — weakest since late 2022
Below the 4.5% forecast and down sharply from 5% in the first quarter
Investment slumped and consumers kept their wallets shut
A 27% June export jump, powered by AI chips and EVs, softened the blow
stories.moot: An economy that grows by building — endless roads, flats and factories — eventually runs out of useful things to build, and growth stalls until spending shifts to consumers. China's struggle shows how hard it is to switch from an investment-led model to one powered by ordinary households, and why a slowdown in one giant economy quietly reprices commodities and shares all over the world.

depth.background: GDP (gross domestic product) is the total value of everything a country produces in a set period — the single broadest gauge of how fast an economy is growing. China is the world's second-largest economy and, for decades, its main growth engine: when China booms it buys the world's oil, metals, machinery and luxury goods, and when it slows the whole planet feels it. But China's old playbook — pouring money into property and infrastructure — has broken down. A years-long housing crash has left developers buried in debt, households nervous about spending, and local governments short of cash. Beijing has leaned harder on exports and high-tech manufacturing (electric vehicles, solar panels, AI-linked electronics) to fill the gap, and set itself a full-year growth target of 4.5-5% — its least ambitious goal in decades.
depth.why_it_matters: When the world's second-biggest economy loses momentum, the effects spill far beyond its borders: China is the largest buyer of many commodities and a huge market for European carmakers, Australian miners and Asian suppliers, so weaker Chinese demand can drag on growth and company profits worldwide. The miss also piles pressure on Beijing to unleash fresh stimulus — extra government spending or interest-rate cuts to jump-start activity — and revives the long-running worry that China is sliding toward a slow-growth, deflationary trap where prices and confidence keep sagging.
depth.ripple_effects: Softer Chinese demand tends to weigh on global commodity prices — crude oil, iron ore, copper — which cuts both ways: it eases costs for importers like India but hurts resource exporters. Expectation of more stimulus can lift Chinese stocks and industrial metals in the short term, while a weaker yuan makes Chinese goods cheaper abroad, intensifying the trade frictions already flaring with the US and Europe. The export strength shows China doubling down on manufacturing the West is trying to tariff, setting up more clashes over 'dumping' cheap EVs and solar panels. And because a big chunk of the slowdown was blamed on higher oil prices from the Iran conflict, it links this data straight back to the Hormuz crisis rattling markets this week.
depth.why_now: The reading is a scheduled quarterly release, but the timing sharpens its meaning: it lands just as the US-Iran war pushes oil to a one-month high — a cost that squeezes energy-hungry China — and as investors debate whether Beijing will finally roll out the big stimulus it has so far avoided. The undershoot against an already-modest target is what turned a routine data drop into a signal that the recovery is stalling.
depth.watch_next: Whether Beijing responds with major new stimulus — extra spending or rate cuts from the People's Bank of China — and the July activity data for early signs the slowdown is deepening or stabilising.
depth.market_link: Watch industrial commodities (crude, copper, iron ore) and China-exposed names — European carmakers, miners like BHP and Rio Tinto, and luxury groups — plus the yuan and Chinese equity indices, which often rise on hopes of fresh stimulus even as the data disappoints.

key_terms.definition: The total value of all goods and services an economy produces in a period; its growth rate is the headline measure of how fast a country is expanding.
Spending on long-lived things like factories, roads, machinery and buildings — a key growth driver in China that is now slumping.
Government or central-bank action — extra spending, tax cuts or lower interest rates — designed to speed up a slowing economy.
A damaging cycle where weak demand pushes prices down, which makes shoppers and firms delay spending, which weakens demand further.

stories.source: CNBC (ASML rises 5% after hiking sales forecast for second time this year on strong AI chip demand)
stories.title: The company that makes the machines that make every advanced AI chip just raised its outlook — again: ASML lifts its 2026 forecast on 'extremely strong' orders
stories.what_happened: On July 15 ASML reported second-quarter net revenue of €9.33 billion (about $10.9 billion), above the €8.8 billion analysts expected, and net profit of €2.92 billion, also a beat. More importantly, it raised its full-year 2026 revenue forecast to €43–45 billion, up roughly 16% at the midpoint from its earlier €36–40 billion — the second time it has lifted guidance this year. Chief executive Christophe Fouquet pointed to 'extremely strong' order intake driven by AI-chip demand, with customers such as TSMC, Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron racing to add capacity. In response, ASML said it will expand both its advanced EUV and its older DUV machine capacity by about 30%. The shares rose around 5%.
key_points: ASML raises 2026 revenue outlook to €43–45bn, up from €36–40bn
Second forecast raise this year; Q2 sales of €9.33bn beat estimates
CEO cites 'extremely strong' orders as chipmakers race to build AI capacity
Shares rose ~5%; ASML will expand its machine-making capacity by 30%
stories.moot: To judge whether a boom is real, look at the base of its supply chain, not the loudest stock. The company selling the one essential tool everyone needs — the 'picks and shovels' of the gold rush — gives a cleaner read on true demand than any single product-maker, because everyone in the industry has to go through it.

depth.background: ASML is a Dutch company most people have never heard of, yet it sits at the very bottom of the entire AI boom. It is effectively the only maker in the world of the most advanced chipmaking machines — 'extreme ultraviolet' (EUV) lithography tools, room-sized systems costing hundreds of millions of dollars each that use light to print circuits just a few atoms wide onto silicon. Every leading-edge chip — the Nvidia processors training AI, the chips inside your phone — is ultimately printed on an ASML machine. Because it has no real competitor at the cutting edge, its order book is the single truest gauge of whether the chip industry genuinely believes AI demand is real, or just hype.
depth.why_it_matters: This is a rare, clean signal about whether the AI build-out is slowing or still accelerating. Individual chip companies can rise or fall on their own quirks, but ASML sells the one essential tool that all of them must buy — so when it raises its outlook twice and calls orders 'extremely strong,' it means the whole industry is still ordering more of the machines needed to make more AI chips. A company adding 30% to its own capacity is telling you it expects that demand to keep growing for years, not months.
depth.ripple_effects: A bullish read from the base of the supply chain tends to lift the entire chip complex — foundries like TSMC, memory makers like SK Hynix (whose shares had already jumped ~13% on AI hopes) and Micron, and the AI-chip designers on top, such as Nvidia. It also lands as a direct counterpoint to IBM's warning days earlier that AI-hardware spending is starving software budgets: ASML's order book confirms that hardware wave is real and physical. The flip side is concentration risk — the world's most important technology depends on a single Dutch firm, which also makes ASML a geopolitical chokepoint, since Washington restricts which of its machines can be sold to China.
depth.why_now: The raise came with ASML's scheduled second-quarter results on July 15, and it reflects a surge in orders in recent months as chipmakers rushed to lock in machines for AI-driven capacity — demand strong enough to justify a second guidance upgrade in a single year.
depth.watch_next: Whether the order momentum holds into the second half of the year, and whether tighter US–China export rules on ASML's machines start to bite into that China demand.
depth.market_link: Bullish for ASML and the whole semiconductor-equipment and memory complex (TSMC, SK Hynix, Micron, Nvidia); it reassures the AI-capex trade and acts as a direct counterpoint to IBM's warning that AI hardware is squeezing software budgets.

key_terms.definition: The process of using extreme-ultraviolet light to 'print' the tiniest circuits onto a chip; ASML is the only company that makes the machines that can do it at the cutting edge.
When a company tells investors it now expects to sell or earn more than it previously said; raising it twice in one year signals demand is running well ahead of plan.
The value of new orders a company takes in; for a supplier like ASML it is a leading indicator — it shows what customers are committing to buy before the products are even built.
Foundries (like TSMC) manufacture chips designed by others; memory makers (like SK Hynix, Micron) make the high-speed memory AI systems need — both are ASML's biggest customers.

sources.name: CNBC (ASML rises 5% after hiking sales forecast for second time this year on strong AI chip demand)
Reuters via Zawya (ASML raises 2026 forecast, expands capacity on AI chip demand)

stories.title: Ann Widdecombe, a well-known British politician, is killed in a 'targeted attack' — and counter-terror police take over the case
stories.what_happened: UK police say Widdecombe was killed in what they are calling a 'targeted attack' — meaning she was deliberately singled out, not harmed at random. A 28-year-old man is in custody and has been re-arrested on suspicion of the commission, preparation or instigation of acts of terrorism, and counter-terrorism officers took over the investigation after receiving 'new information'. Police say they have not formally declared it a terrorist incident but are still working to establish a motive, and have indicated they are examining a possible far-left (leftwing) political motivation.
key_points: Ann Widdecombe, ex-UK MP and Reform party figure, killed in a 'targeted attack'
Counter-terrorism police take the lead; a 28-year-old suspect re-arrested on terror suspicion
Not yet declared terrorism; police probing a possible far-left motive
Her party, Reform UK, demands 'full security' for all lawmakers
stories.moot: Political violence is measured not just by the one life lost but by the fear it spreads — when a public figure is attacked, the real target is open debate itself, which is why states treat such acts as terrorism rather than ordinary crime.

depth.background: Ann Widdecombe is a familiar figure in British public life — a former Conservative Member of Parliament who later became a leading voice on the pro-Brexit right and a member of Nigel Farage's Reform UK party, as well as a long-time television personality. Her killing lands in a decade already scarred by the murders of two sitting MPs — Jo Cox in 2016 and Sir David Amess in 2021 — which turned the safety of politicians into a raw national issue.
depth.why_it_matters: The killing of a prominent politician is an attack on open democracy itself: it can intimidate public figures and chill ordinary political life. The fact that counter-terror police are leading, and the suspect is held on terrorism suspicion, raises the possibility this was politically motivated violence rather than a random crime — which is why the state is treating it so seriously.
depth.ripple_effects: Reform UK, the party she belonged to, is demanding 'full security' for all lawmakers, which could mean more protection details, tighter rules around public appearances, and renewed debate about online radicalisation. It reopens Britain's decade-long argument about how to keep elected officials safe after Cox and Amess. And if a partisan motive is confirmed, it risks sharpening left-right tensions and piling pressure on the government over how it counters home-grown extremism.
depth.why_now: The case escalated between 13 and 15 July as counter-terror police took the lead 'after new information', re-arrested the suspect on terrorism grounds, and signalled they are examining a possible political motive — turning a shocking crime into a national-security story that is dominating UK headlines.
depth.watch_next: Whether police formally declare it a terrorist act and what they conclude about the motive — the crucial fork between a lone crime and political terrorism.

key_terms.definition: Police wording meaning the victim was deliberately singled out, not hurt at random.
A specialist UK police command that takes over cases where terrorism — violence for a political, religious or ideological cause — is suspected.
A right-wing British political party led by Nigel Farage that grew out of the Brexit movement.

stories.source: Reuters (China's Q2 economic growth cools to 3-1/2-year low as imbalances worsen)
stories.title: China's economy grows just 4.3% — its slowest pace since 2022 and its first missed target since Covid, as weak demand at home offsets a booming export machine
stories.what_happened: Official data released July 15 showed China's economy expanded 4.3% in the April–June quarter from a year earlier, slowing from roughly 5% at the start of 2026 and marking its weakest quarterly growth in more than three years. Crucially, it came in below Beijing's own full-year target range of 4.5–5% — described as the least ambitious goal in decades yet still missed — the first time China has fallen short since the Covid era. The shortfall was driven by the domestic side: fixed-asset investment (spending on things like factories, machinery and construction) slumped and consumer demand stayed soft, even as exports surged. Higher oil prices from the Iran war added to the drag. Economists said the numbers strengthen the case for fresh stimulus.
key_points: April–June GDP grew 4.3% from a year earlier, down from ~5% at the start of the year
That undershoots Beijing's own 4.5–5% full-year target — its first miss since the pandemic
Slumping investment and soft consumer spending, not exports, dragged growth down
The weak read is fuelling calls for fresh government stimulus
stories.moot: Strong headline exports can hide a weak economy underneath. Growth has two engines — what a country sells abroad and what its own people and firms spend at home — and when the domestic engine stalls, foreign sales alone can't keep the whole machine at full speed. A missed official target is often the first public admission that the weakness is real.

depth.background: China is the world's second-largest economy and its biggest manufacturer, so how fast it grows sets the pace for global demand — for everything from iron ore and copper to the ships and factories that move world trade. Each year Beijing sets an official GDP growth target; hitting it is treated as a point of national credibility, and the government has almost always met it. GDP ('gross domestic product') is simply the total value of everything a country produces in a period, and China reports it quarterly. For two years its story has been lopsided: a powerful export sector — lately supercharged by the global AI hardware boom — masking a weak home economy weighed down by a property slump and cautious consumers.
depth.why_it_matters: This is the clearest sign yet that China's growth engine is running on one cylinder. Its export boom is real, but you cannot power a $19-trillion economy on foreign sales alone when your own households and companies aren't spending or investing — and a missed target signals Beijing knows it. Because China buys a huge share of the world's raw materials and industrial goods, a genuine slowdown there ripples out to commodity exporters, global manufacturers and anyone whose business depends on Chinese demand.
depth.ripple_effects: A cooling China tends to soften prices for industrial commodities — oil, copper, iron ore — which cuts revenue for resource exporters like Australia and Brazil but can ease input costs (and inflation) for importers like India. It pressures the Chinese yuan and China-exposed multinationals, while raising the odds of government stimulus — extra spending or interest-rate cuts — that could later lift Chinese stocks and demand. A weaker China also means fiercer competition abroad, as its factories chase overseas buyers to make up for thin demand at home, feeding trade tensions with the US and Europe.
depth.why_now: The second-quarter GDP figures landed July 15 on their scheduled release, and they crystallised a slowdown that had been building all year — the property drag, timid consumers and now a fresh oil-price hit from the Iran war finally pulling headline growth below the official goal.
depth.watch_next: Whether Beijing responds with a concrete stimulus package (extra spending or rate cuts) in the coming weeks, and whether retail sales and investment data stabilise or keep sliding.
depth.market_link: A China slowdown weighs on industrial commodities (oil, copper, iron ore), the yuan and China-exposed stocks, while stoking stimulus hopes that could later buoy Chinese equities; a drag for commodity-exporter currencies but a potential easing of import costs for buyers like India.

key_terms.definition: The total value of all goods and services an economy produces in a period; its growth rate is the headline gauge of whether an economy is speeding up or slowing down.
The annual GDP growth figure China's government sets as its official goal; meeting it is a matter of credibility, and Beijing has rarely missed — until now.
Money spent on long-lived things like factories, machinery, roads and buildings; a slump here means businesses and local governments are holding back, a sign of weak confidence.
Steps a government or central bank takes to boost a slowing economy — extra public spending, tax cuts or lower interest rates to encourage borrowing and spending.

sources.name: Reuters (China's Q2 economic growth cools to 3-1/2-year low as imbalances worsen)
CNBC (China posts slowest quarterly growth since 2022 as investment slumps, fanning stimulus calls)
AP News (China's economy slows to 4.3% annual pace of growth in April-June)
BBC (China economic growth falls sharply, missing target)

stories.title: France legalises assisted dying: after 14 years of debate, the National Assembly passes a law letting terminally ill adults take lethal medication
stories.what_happened: In the early hours of July 15, 2026, France's National Assembly gave final approval to the assisted-dying bill by 291 votes to 241. The law lets adults (18+) who are French citizens or legal residents, and who have a serious, incurable, life-threatening illness in an advanced or terminal stage with suffering that cannot be relieved, request lethal medication. Patients must have the mental capacity to make the choice and must confirm it after a reflection period of at least two days; they generally self-administer the drug, with a doctor or nurse assisting only if they are physically unable. Psychological suffering alone does not qualify, and people with severe psychiatric disorders or neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's are excluded.
key_points: France's lower house passed the bill 291–241, ending a 14-year fight
Incurable, advanced-stage adults can get lethal medication under strict rules
The Senate rejected it, but the National Assembly has the final say
It still needs the Constitutional Council's sign-off to take effect
stories.moot: Big social change often turns on procedure as much as persuasion: when a two-chamber legislature deadlocks, the rules about which house gets the 'final say' can decide an outcome the moral argument alone never settled.

depth.background: Whether the state should let a doctor help a dying person end their life is one of the oldest, most divisive questions in medical ethics — pitting personal autonomy and mercy against fears that the vulnerable, elderly or disabled could be pushed toward death. France has argued over it for more than a decade across successive governments, with reformers pointing to neighbours who already allow it and opponents (including much of the Catholic church and some doctors' groups) warning of a slippery slope. The bill had already cleared the National Assembly in earlier readings but kept hitting resistance in the Senate, France's upper house.
depth.why_it_matters: France is one of Europe's largest countries, so its choice reshapes the continent's map on end-of-life rights and gives momentum to reformers elsewhere. It joins a still-small club — the Netherlands, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada and several US states — that permit some form of assisted dying, moving a deeply personal medical decision from the shadows into a regulated legal framework.
depth.ripple_effects: Hospitals, doctors and nurses now face the practical work of writing rules, training, conscientious-objection protections and safeguards to prevent abuse. Expect renewed fights over funding for palliative (comfort) care, which critics say must be strong enough that no one chooses death for lack of alternatives. The vote will also energise campaigners — on both sides — in countries like the UK, Germany and Italy where similar bills are being debated, and religious institutions are likely to keep contesting it.
depth.why_now: The bill reached this point because the National Assembly used its constitutional power to have the final word after the Senate rejected the text — a procedural route that let reformers push it through despite the deadlock, capping 14 years of stop-start debate.
depth.watch_next: France's Constitutional Council, which has up to a month to rule on whether the law is constitutional; the law only takes effect once it clears that review.

key_terms.definition: A legal arrangement in which a terminally or incurably ill person can be given medication to end their own life, under strict medical and legal conditions.
France's highest constitutional authority, which checks whether a new law complies with the constitution before it can take effect.

stories.title: The fastest-spreading Ebola outbreak ever is raging in DR Congo — 2,000+ cases and 754 dead, and there's no vaccine that works on this strain
stories.what_happened: The World Health Organization (WHO) declared this outbreak a Public Health Emergency of International Concern — its highest global alarm — on May 17, 2026. As of mid-July, the DRC had recorded 2,011 confirmed cases and 754 deaths across five provinces (Ituri, Haut-Uele, North Kivu, South Kivu and Tshopo), with imported cases reaching Uganda's capital, Kampala. The outbreak crossed 1,000 confirmed cases in just 40 days — a 2018 DRC outbreak took about 235 days to reach the same point — making it the third-largest Ebola epidemic on record and the fastest-growing yet. Because the Bundibugyo strain has no proven vaccine, responders are falling back on the classic tools: isolating the sick, tracing everyone they contacted, and conducting safe burials.
key_points: DR Congo reports 2,011 cases and 754 deaths as of mid-July
Hit 1,000 cases in 40 days — the 2018 outbreak took 235
Caused by the Bundibugyo strain, which has NO licensed vaccine or cure
Already reached Uganda's capital Kampala; WHO calls it a global emergency
stories.moot: A medical breakthrough only protects you against the exact problem it was built for. The world has powerful Ebola vaccines — but they target one strain, so a different strain can leave responders back at square one. Preparedness isn't a single tool; it's having tools for each version of a threat, and a fast, trusted public-health system to use them.

depth.background: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal disease that spreads through contact with the blood and body fluids of an infected person, causing fever, bleeding and organ failure. The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), a vast, poor country in central Africa with a weak health system and ongoing armed conflict in its east, has suffered more Ebola outbreaks than anywhere on Earth — this is its 17th. Crucially, there are several different types ('strains') of Ebola virus, and the two vaccines the world has stockpiled were built to fight the most common one, the Zaire strain. This outbreak is caused by a different, rarer strain — Bundibugyo — for which no vaccine or drug has ever been licensed.
depth.why_it_matters: This is Ebola without its most powerful modern weapon. In recent outbreaks, ring-vaccination (rapidly vaccinating everyone around a case) helped choke the virus; here that option doesn't exist, so containment depends entirely on slow, labour-intensive detective work in a conflict zone. The speed of spread and the jump into a capital city of over 1.5 million people raise the risk of the outbreak escaping into densely populated areas and across borders — which is exactly why the WHO rang its loudest alarm.
depth.ripple_effects: Neighbouring countries and airports are tightening screening — the US has already stiffened travel measures, and single infected travellers have turned up as far away as Germany, showing how one plane ride can carry the virus between continents. Aid agencies must pour scarce staff and money into contact-tracing and treatment centres, straining a response already competing with the DRC's wars and other emergencies. Drugmakers and health agencies face fresh pressure to develop and test a Bundibugyo-specific vaccine, since the world's existing shots may not protect against it. And every week the outbreak grows, the economic and human toll on eastern Congo — trade, schooling, farming — deepens.
depth.why_now: The outbreak began quietly with early infections around the town of Mongbwalu in January–February 2026, then accelerated sharply through the spring and summer; the surge past 2,000 cases and 750 deaths, combined with the spread to Kampala and new travel restrictions abroad, is what has pushed it back into global headlines this week.
depth.watch_next: Whether health teams can bend the curve with contact-tracing alone, whether Uganda stays free of new cases (its last was in early June), and whether any Bundibugyo-targeted vaccine is fast-tracked for emergency use.
depth.market_link: Not a major market mover, but a widening outbreak keeps attention on Ebola-vaccine makers (Merck, which makes Ervebo, and Bavarian Nordic) and could spur funding for a Bundibugyo-specific shot; a broader scare would also lift diagnostics and protective-equipment suppliers.

key_terms.definition: A rarer type of the Ebola virus; unlike the common Zaire strain, it has no licensed vaccine or specific treatment, so outbreaks must be fought with isolation and contact-tracing.

key_terms.term: Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC)
key_terms.definition: The World Health Organization's highest level of alarm, declared when a disease threatens to spread across borders and needs a coordinated global response.

Ring vaccination means quickly vaccinating everyone around a confirmed case to box the virus in; contact tracing means finding and monitoring every person an infected patient met. Without a working vaccine, only tracing is available here.

stories.title: After 23 years, America is leaving Iraq: Washington and Baghdad say every US troop will be gone by September 30
stories.what_happened: Standing beside Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi at the White House on Tuesday, President Trump said all remaining US forces will leave Iraq by September 30, declaring 'we don't think we need the military there anymore' and pointing to Iraq's growing ties with American oil companies. Al-Zaidi framed it as 'US forces will be out of Iraq' by Sept 30 'while US companies will be inside Iraq' — a swap of soldiers for business. The Pentagon confirmed it, saying the move simply reaffirms the 2024 agreement to end the anti-ISIS mission now that the group is largely defeated.
key_points: US and Iraq agree: all American forces out by Sept 30, 2026
Ends a military presence that began with the 2003 invasion
Trump: 'we don't think we need the military there anymore'
Comes as the US-Iran war puts US bases in Iraq in the crossfire
stories.moot: Military footprints are political, not permanent: once the original mission fades — here, defeating ISIS — the justification for staying erodes. But pulling out rarely ends a country's problems; it usually just hands influence to whoever is willing to step into the gap.

depth.background: The United States invaded Iraq in March 2003 with a 'shock and awe' bombing campaign that toppled dictator Saddam Hussein, and American troops have been in the country in some form ever since — first fighting a long insurgency, later helping Iraq crush the Islamic State (ISIS), the extremist group that seized huge parts of Iraq and Syria around 2014. As ISIS was beaten back, the mission shrank to a small training-and-advising role of roughly 2,500 troops, and in 2024 Washington and Baghdad already signed a deal to wind that mission down. This new announcement sets a hard finish line for a 23-year chapter.
depth.why_it_matters: This closes one of the longest and most consequential US military commitments of the century, and it recasts the US-Iraq relationship from a security partnership into an economic one built around oil and investment. It also removes American 'boots on the ground' from a country wedged between Iran and the Gulf at the exact moment the US is fighting an open war with Iran — which cuts both ways: fewer US targets for Iran-backed militias, but also less US ability to shape events on Iran's doorstep.
depth.ripple_effects: A US pullout leaves a power vacuum that Iran, whose allied Shiite militias are deeply embedded inside Iraq, is best placed to fill — raising the risk that Baghdad drifts further into Tehran's orbit. Iraq's oil sector, a major OPEC supplier, becomes the new center of gravity for US influence, so American energy firms and their contracts stand to gain. Security analysts will watch for any ISIS resurgence once US forces and their surveillance leave, and for how the withdrawal reshapes the wider Iran war, since Iraqi and Israeli bases there have already been drawn into the fighting.
depth.why_now: The 2024 US-Iraq deal always pointed toward an exit, but the timing is being forced by two things at once: Trump's drive to pull American troops out of Middle East conflicts, and the ongoing US-Iran war, which has turned bases in Iraq into flashpoints for Iran-aligned militia attacks — making a continued presence both riskier and harder to justify.
depth.watch_next: Whether the September 30 deadline actually holds — and what happens to Iraq's security, ISIS activity, and Iran-backed militia behavior once the last US troops depart.
depth.market_link: Iraq is a top-five OPEC oil producer, so a US pivot from troops to energy deals — during a live Iran war — keeps a risk premium under crude prices and puts Western oil majors operating in Iraq in focus.

key_terms.definition: A hardline extremist group that seized large parts of Iraq and Syria around 2014; the US-led mission in Iraq was built around defeating it.
Armed Iraqi groups, many backed and armed by Iran, that operate alongside — and sometimes against — the official Iraqi state and US forces.
When a departing power (here, the US military) leaves a gap that rival forces rush to fill.

stories.source: NDTV (Vietnam boat tragedy: PM Modi condoles deaths of Indian tourists)
stories.title: 15 Indian tourists killed as a boat capsizes off Vietnam's Phu Quoc — Modi condoles, Hanoi orders a probe
stories.what_happened: A tourist boat carrying Indian visitors capsized in waters near Phu Quoc, killing at least 15 people — 13 men and 2 women, according to Indian reporting. A survivor recounted that the vessel 'flipped in three minutes,' describing the captain jumping into the water before he did. The remains of victims from Tamil Nadu and Kerala are being repatriated. Vietnamese authorities have ordered a probe, and the Phu Quoc administration has rejected allegations that the rescue was delayed.
key_points: At least 15 Indian tourists — 13 men, 2 women — died when their boat capsized.
A survivor says the boat 'flipped in three minutes' near Phu Quoc island.
PM Modi condoled the deaths; Vietnam ordered an investigation.
Families in Tamil Nadu and Kerala grieve as bodies are flown home.
stories.moot: Tourism safety is only as strong as its least-regulated operator: when a destination gets popular fast, visitor numbers can outrun the rules for boats, buses and guides — so one lapse can produce an outsized, cross-border tragedy.

depth.background: Phu Quoc is Vietnam's largest island, a southern resort destination that has become increasingly popular with Indian tourists for holidays and group trips, with boat excursions a common activity. The victims in this incident were largely from Tamil Nadu and Kerala.
depth.why_it_matters: It is one of the deadliest single incidents involving Indian tourists abroad in recent memory, turning an ordinary holiday into a national tragedy and raising urgent questions about the safety standards of tourist-boat operators at a fast-growing destination.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect tighter scrutiny of Vietnam's tourist-boat safety and licensing, consular strain as India identifies victims and repatriates remains, and more caution among Indian travellers and tour operators toward such excursions. The grief is concentrated in Tamil Nadu and Kerala, where local leaders are already paying tributes, and disputes over the rescue response could sharpen diplomatic sensitivity.
depth.why_now: Phu Quoc's rising popularity with Indian tourists means more Indians are taking these boat trips than ever, so a single operator's safety failure now carries a far larger Indian toll — and the story is peaking as bodies return home and survivors begin to speak.
depth.watch_next: The findings of Vietnam's investigation — whether overloading, weather, or operator negligence caused the capsize — and whether any charges follow against the boat operator.

key_terms.definition: Vietnam's largest island, a southern resort destination increasingly popular with international tourists, including from India.
The process of returning a deceased person's body to their home country, coordinated between governments and the families.
The help a country's embassy gives its citizens in an emergency abroad — here, identifying victims, aiding families, and arranging the return of remains.

sources.name: NDTV (Vietnam boat tragedy: PM Modi condoles deaths of Indian tourists)
The Times of India (Vietnam boat survivor: 'Captain jumped, so did I')

stories.title: New York becomes the first US state to hit pause on the AI data-center boom: Gov. Hochul signs a one-year freeze on giant new server farms
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed an executive order barring construction of new large-scale data centers that draw 50 megawatts of power or more — as much as a small town uses — for up to one year. The order pauses state permitting for such projects and directs regulators to draw up new standards on energy demand, water use and environmental impact. A separate, tougher bill in the legislature (the Responsible Data Center Development Act) would freeze projects above 20 megawatts; Hochul has said she will 'further review' it before deciding whether to sign.
key_points: Executive order freezes new 'hyperscale' data centers (50+ megawatts) for up to a year
First US state to slam the brakes on the AI building spree
Trigger: soaring electricity bills and anger over power and water use
Regulators told to write new rules on energy, water and environmental impact
stories.moot: Every digital boom eventually collides with the physical world. AI runs on electricity, water and land — so the real limit on how fast it grows may turn out to be power grids and the voters who pay the bills, not chips or money.

depth.background: Training and running AI models needs enormous banks of computers — 'data centers' — that guzzle electricity and water to stay cool. Since the AI boom took off, tech giants have raced to build ever-bigger ones ('hyperscale' campuses), and the extra demand has started pushing up power bills and straining local grids across the US. New York, like many states, had been courting these projects for the jobs and investment — until the backlash grew louder than the sales pitch.
depth.why_it_matters: This is the first time a US state has told the AI-infrastructure gold rush to stop and wait. The entire AI trade — the chips, the cloud, the trillion-dollar spending plans — rests on an assumption that data centers can be built as fast as demand grows. A big, wealthy state saying 'not so fast' cracks that assumption and hands other states and communities a template to do the same.
depth.ripple_effects: Tech firms planning New York campuses now face delays and uncertainty, and may divert projects to friendlier states. For ordinary New Yorkers, the pause is meant to shield them from data centers bidding up electricity prices and draining water. For the AI industry, it raises the risk that power and permits — not chips — become the real bottleneck. Utilities, grid operators, data-center landlords and chipmakers all read this as a warning that the physical world (electricity, water, local politics) can slow AI down.
depth.why_now: Voters are opening electricity bills that have jumped as data centers soak up power, and opposition has spread from local zoning fights to statewide politics — so an election-conscious governor acted.
depth.watch_next: Whether Hochul signs the tougher legislative bill above 20 megawatts, and whether other states — or countries — copy New York's moratorium.
depth.market_link: A state-level brake on the AI buildout pressures the whole 'AI infrastructure' trade — data-center landlords (Equinix, Digital Realty), power and utility stocks, grid-equipment makers, and ultimately chipmakers like Nvidia whose sales depend on new server farms actually getting built.

key_terms.definition: A large building full of computer servers that store data and run online services — including the AI models behind chatbots.
The very largest class of data center, run by big cloud and AI companies, using vast amounts of power, water and space.
A unit of electrical power; a single 50-megawatt data center can draw as much electricity as tens of thousands of homes.
A temporary official ban or pause on an activity.

stories.title: Supreme Court flags Patna HC order that pressing a woman's chest is 'not attempt to rape'
stories.what_happened: During that ongoing hearing, senior advocates Shobha Gupta and H.S. Phoolka told a three-judge Supreme Court bench — led by Chief Justice Surya Kant, with Justices Joymalya Bagchi and V. Mohana — that a Patna High Court had recently passed a strikingly similar order. In a 2008 case from a photo studio in Amarpur, Bihar, the Patna HC held that confining a woman, pressing her chest and trying to remove her salwar amounted only to 'outraging her modesty' under Section 354, not attempt to rape, because there was no penetration or clear overt act pointing to rape. The CJI said the bench would 'pass a detailed order dealing with the Patna High Court decision.'
key_points: Patna HC: pressing chest, pulling at salwar = 'outraging modesty', not attempt to rape
Top court's CJI-led bench says it will pass a detailed order on the ruling
Echoes a 2025 Allahabad HC order the SC already set aside
At stake: the legal line between molestation and attempted rape
stories.moot: In law, the label on a crime decides the punishment — so the exact test a court uses to sort one offence from another can matter as much as the facts. Higher courts exist partly to keep those tests consistent, so that similar acts don't get wildly different sentences depending on which judge hears them.

depth.background: In India, sexual-assault crimes are graded, and the grade decides the punishment. 'Outraging the modesty of a woman' (Section 354 of the old penal code) is a lesser offence carrying a few years in jail; 'attempt to rape' is treated almost as seriously as rape itself and can mean many years to life. So how a court labels an act — molestation versus attempted rape — massively changes the sentence.
