Hiring an outside company — often in India — to run part of your technology or business operations more cheaply.

stories.title: First-ever treatment trial for this Ebola strain begins at record speed
stories.what_happened: The WHO-backed PARTNERS trial — short for a 'platform adaptive randomised trial', a flexible study that can test several drugs at once and add or drop them as data arrives — enrolled its first patients in the DRC, testing two candidates: MBP134, a lab-made antibody designed to neutralise the virus, and remdesivir, an antiviral. It was set up in about six weeks, a record for this kind of research, after the WHO declared the outbreak a 'public health emergency of international concern' on 17 May. Gilead donated the remdesivir and the US government the antibody — enough for up to 1,200 patients. The outbreak is the largest Bundibugyo episode on record, with roughly 454 deaths among about 1,481 cases.
key_points: WHO-backed PARTNERS trial enrolls first patients against a live outbreak
Two drugs tested: antibody MBP134 and antiviral remdesivir
Stood up in just ~6 weeks after WHO declared a global emergency
Outbreak has killed ~454; no drug has ever been proven for this strain
stories.moot: Having a drug is not the same as knowing it works; without controlled trials, medicine is guesswork — so the ability to run a rigorous study fast, in the middle of a crisis, is itself a life-saving capability.

thread.previously: On July 1 we reported this DRC Ebola outbreak had passed 300 deaths and was on track to be one of the deadliest in recent history, and flagged vaccination and contact-tracing as the things to watch.
thread.changed: The toll has since climbed to around 454 deaths, the strain is confirmed as Bundibugyo, and the response has shifted from containment toward treatment: a first-ever, record-fast drug trial for this strain has begun enrolling patients.

depth.background: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal virus spread through the bodily fluids of infected people; it causes internal bleeding and organ failure and was first identified near the DRC's Ebola River in 1976. There are several species of the virus. Two approved treatments exist — but only for the Zaire species. The current outbreak is the Bundibugyo species, which has sickened people since 2007 and against which no drug has ever been formally tested. Running such a trial is hard because outbreaks are remote, fast-moving and dangerous.
depth.why_it_matters: For the first time, doctors fighting this strain of Ebola may get hard evidence about what actually works, instead of guessing. Proven treatments sharply cut death rates and, crucially, give people a reason to come to clinics early rather than hide — which itself helps stop the spread. Doing it in six weeks shows the world's outbreak-response machinery has become far faster since COVID.
depth.ripple_effects: If either drug works, it could become a stockpiled standard treatment for future Bundibugyo outbreaks across central Africa, and the speed 'playbook' could be reused for the next emerging disease. It also underscores how much this response leans on donated drugs and Western agencies — support that can be fragile when budgets or politics shift.
depth.why_now: The trial launched now because the outbreak is both large enough to enroll patients and severe enough to justify emergency approvals — and responders deliberately compressed the setup after the mid-May global-emergency declaration to reach patients while the outbreak is still active.
depth.watch_next: Early trial read-outs on whether MBP134 or remdesivir reduces deaths, and whether new case numbers start to fall.
depth.market_link: Minimal — Gilead (GILD), maker of remdesivir, donated its drug here; outbreaks like this rarely move markets unless they threaten to spread internationally.

key_terms.definition: A carefully controlled study in real patients that tests whether a treatment is safe and actually works, usually by comparing people who get it with those who don't.
A lab-made copy of an immune-system protein, designed to lock onto a specific virus and help the body destroy it.

key_terms.term: Public health emergency of international concern
key_terms.definition: The WHO's highest alarm level for a disease outbreak, meant to speed up funding, research and cooperation across countries.

stories.title: China evacuates nearly two million as Typhoon Bavi hits Zhejiang
stories.what_happened: Typhoon Bavi made landfall near the city of Yuhuan in Zhejiang province late Saturday, weakening from a full typhoon to a tropical storm but remaining enormous. Authorities evacuated more than 2.2 million people in Zhejiang alone, plus over 180,000 in neighbouring Fujian and roughly 290,000 in Shanghai, and suspended schools, work and much transport. The storm felled more than 1,300 trees in Yueqing, and cancelled hundreds of flights and over 1,600 trains across the region.
key_points: ~2.2 million evacuated in Zhejiang; hundreds of thousands more nearby
Bavi weakened to a tropical storm at landfall but stayed vast
Rain bands ~1,000km wide — roughly the length of France
Hundreds of flights and 1,600+ trains cancelled
stories.moot: Warmer oceans hand storms more energy, so the same weather system can grow larger and wetter than it once would have. And the smartest way to survive a big storm is to move people before it arrives — the cost of relocating millions is far smaller than the cost of not.

thread.previously: Over several days we tracked Bavi crossing Taiwan and nearing landfall in Zhejiang, with evacuations scaled up toward two million and 15 killed earlier in Philippine landslides.
thread.changed: Bavi has now come ashore near Yuhuan and Wenzhou, weakening from a typhoon to a tropical storm; final tallies show 2.2 million evacuated in Zhejiang plus hundreds of thousands more in Fujian and Shanghai, with mass flight and train cancellations.

depth.background: The western Pacific is in the peak of typhoon season — typhoons are the same kind of storm the Atlantic calls a hurricane. Warmer ocean water is the fuel these storms feed on, so hotter seas tend to make them bigger, wetter and more intense. Bavi struck China's densely populated, factory-heavy east coast — a region that sits near Taiwan, home to the world's most advanced computer-chip factories — and was the country's second major typhoon in a single week.
depth.why_it_matters: A giant storm over China's manufacturing coast is an economic risk, not just a weather one — big typhoons can shut ports, factories and rail, and any brush with Taiwan threatens the chip supply chain behind global electronics and AI hardware. The scale of the evacuation — nearly two million people moved before the storm hit — is also a stress test of emergency systems, and back-to-back storms compound the strain on people and infrastructure.
depth.ripple_effects: Halted ports and factories ripple into shipping schedules and supply chains far beyond China; days of heavy rain raise the risk of deadly inland flooding and landslides after the winds pass. Mass evacuations and infrastructure damage drive up disaster-relief and insurance costs. If a future storm on this track were to disrupt Taiwan's chip fabs or China's coastal industry, the effect would be felt in electronics prices worldwide.
depth.why_now: Peak typhoon season has produced two storms in a week, and Bavi's exceptional size forced pre-emptive mass evacuations as it bore down on a heavily populated coast.
depth.watch_next: Whether Bavi's inland rain triggers serious flooding, and whether coastal ports, factories or transport suffer lasting disruption.
depth.market_link: A watch item for chip and electronics supply: a serious hit to Taiwan's fabs or China's coastal ports and factories would ripple into global electronics and AI-hardware costs, and lift Asian insurers' claims.

key_terms.definition: A powerful rotating storm that forms over warm tropical seas in the western Pacific — the same phenomenon called a hurricane in the Atlantic.
A rotating storm weaker than a typhoon but still capable of destructive winds and heavy, flooding rain.
Moving people out of a danger zone before a disaster strikes, to prevent casualties rather than react to them.

stories.title: Apple sues OpenAI, alleging a stolen-secrets shortcut into AI hardware
stories.what_happened: Apple filed a lawsuit in a US federal court in Northern California accusing OpenAI of stealing 'trade secrets' — confidential know-how that gives a company its edge. Apple says the scheme reached 'every level' and was directed by OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan, who spent 24 years at Apple, most recently as VP of product design for the iPhone and Watch. Among the claims: Tan urged Apple staff he was recruiting to bring 'actual parts' from Apple to their interviews for 'show and tell'; he emailed himself details of Apple's suppliers before leaving; departing staff were allegedly coached to dodge Apple's exit-security checks; and one former employee is accused of stealing an Apple laptop. Apple wants damages and a court order forcing OpenAI to stop using the secrets. OpenAI has not conceded the claims, which remain untested in court.
key_points: Apple accuses OpenAI of poaching staff to steal iPhone-making secrets
Central figure: OpenAI hardware chief Tang Tan, a 24-year Apple veteran
Claims include recruits bringing real Apple parts to job interviews
Two AI-era titans now in open court, not just competition
stories.moot: A company's real 'moat' is often not a single patent but the accumulated know-how in its people and processes — which is exactly why talent wars and trade-secret suits, not just product launches, help decide who wins a technology race.

depth.background: Apple, the world's most valuable company, built its fortune on tightly-guarded hardware — the iPhone, Watch and AirPods. OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, has been racing beyond software into physical devices: in 2025 it bought io, the startup founded by former Apple design legend Jony Ive, and hired dozens of ex-Apple engineers to build an AI-first gadget. That hiring spree has now curdled into a courtroom fight.
depth.why_it_matters: This is two of the most powerful companies of the AI era squaring off in open legal conflict, not merely competing. Trade-secret law protects the things patents don't cover — manufacturing tricks, supplier lists, design processes — and a fight this public signals both how valuable Apple's hardware playbook is and how badly OpenAI wants to shortcut years of learning to get a device into your pocket.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect a chilling effect on the freewheeling job-hopping between Big Tech and AI labs — tighter contracts, longer 'garden-leave' waiting periods, and lawyers vetting senior hires. If Apple wins or forces a settlement, OpenAI's hardware timeline could slip. It also spotlights how much of America's tech edge lives inside employees' heads, and how easily it can walk out the door.
depth.why_now: OpenAI's hardware push has accelerated since it absorbed Jony Ive's io team, and the wave of ex-Apple hires reached a point where Apple decided the pattern looked like theft, not coincidence — so it moved to court to try to stop the device before it ships.
depth.watch_next: OpenAI's formal response, and whether the judge grants any early injunction limiting what OpenAI's ex-Apple staff can work on.
depth.market_link: Watch Apple (AAPL) for headline sensitivity and any read-through to how aggressively it will defend its hardware IP; OpenAI is privately held, so the market impact here is mostly reputational and strategic.

key_terms.definition: Confidential business information — a recipe, supplier list or design process — that a company protects and that gives it an edge; stealing it can be illegal even without a patent.
A court order telling someone to stop doing something — here, using disputed information — while or after a case is decided.

stories.title: India's most famous central banker lands a US seat: ex-RBI chief Raghuram Rajan is co-leading a task force to overhaul the Federal Reserve
stories.what_happened: On July 9 the new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, announced five expert 'task forces' to overhaul how the Fed works — covering its communications, its balance sheet, its use of economic data, jobs and productivity, and its inflation framework. Rajan was named a co-chair of the balance-sheet task force alongside Harvard economist Karen Dynan and former Fed governor Jeremy Stein. Indian outlets reported that Rajan is among a small group of Indian-origin economists Warsh has drawn on for the review.
key_points: Ex-RBI Governor Raghuram Rajan tapped for a US Fed reform panel
He co-chairs the group rethinking the Fed's balance sheet
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has launched five task forces
Findings due later in 2026 could reshape global interest rates
stories.moot: A central bank's balance sheet is a hidden throttle on the world's money supply, and because US interest rates anchor global capital, who sits on the Fed's review panels can matter to a saver in Mumbai almost as much as to one in Chicago.

depth.background: Raghuram Rajan ran India's central bank, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), from 2013 to 2016 — taming double-digit inflation, steadying a sliding rupee, and helping put India on a modern rate-setting footing. Earlier, as chief economist of the IMF, he famously warned in 2005 that the financial system was becoming dangerously risky, three years before the 2008 crash. He is now a finance professor at the University of Chicago. The US Federal Reserve — America's central bank, whose interest-rate moves ripple through every market on earth — is now rethinking how it operates after inflation ran above its target for more than five years.
depth.why_it_matters: The 'balance sheet' is the Fed's giant hoard of bonds — how big it is decides how much money sloshes through the global financial system. Having Rajan help redraw those rules means an Indian-trained economist now has real influence over decisions that set the cost of money worldwide, India included. It is also a marker of Indian soft power: the man who ran Mumbai's Mint Street is now advising Washington's.
depth.ripple_effects: If the task forces push for a smaller Fed balance sheet or a tougher line on inflation, US interest rates could stay higher for longer — which tends to strengthen the dollar, pressure the rupee, and pull foreign money (FII flows) out of Indian stocks and bonds. Rajan, a long-standing critic of ultra-loose money, may lean that way. For Indian readers it is also a reminder of how tightly the RBI's own room to cut rates is bound to whatever the Fed does.
depth.why_now: The story is resurfacing in Indian media this week as the task-force line-ups are digested at home — and because the Fed is moving fast: preliminary findings are reportedly expected around September, with final recommendations by the end of 2026.
depth.watch_next: The task forces' first findings, expected around September, for early hints on whether the Fed will shrink its balance sheet or harden its inflation target.
depth.market_link: Fed balance-sheet and inflation-framework changes steer US rates and the dollar — a hawkish shift would pressure the rupee, lift Indian bond yields, and slow FII flows into Indian equities.

key_terms.definition: India's central bank — it sets interest rates, manages the rupee, and regulates the country's banks.
The pile of bonds and assets a central bank owns; growing it pumps money into the economy, shrinking it drains money out.
Money that foreign institutional investors move in and out of a country's stocks and bonds — big inflows lift markets, outflows drag them down.
